entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: You would like to start telling me about the overall context of Postovon and how you managed to contact them and get the interview wonderful. Yes, I can start describing that so Postobón is one of the largest companies in Colombia. They have been around for a few decades already. I also read somewhere that they were one of the biggest companies in Latin America. As well, they started producing soft carbonated drinks so sodas. And this is still the main part of their business. Although they have started to produce also juices and even condiments and sauces and other things. They explained to us that what we call Postavon is actually a group of twelve companies and it includes many things. It includes a transport company, it includes a company that does reforestation. It includes several things. The way we contacted the Initiative was through LinkedIn. I reached out to the sustainability director. I didn't know her, but it turns out we had some connections in common because she was the environment secretary of Sergio Fajardo, the former mayor of Medellín, and I worked with Antanas Motzkus. And they worked together. So we had several people in common and she took a while. But she was very enthusiastic to answer and to agree to an interview still maybe in methodological terms. I think I learned with this case that these meso cases have to be treated a little bit different than the smaller cases that we have done. You have to be much more careful. They are still interested on being interviewed by European Institute and this is a major motivation. But they also need some reassurance that you are not going to sell their data or to use it for whatever purpose. That they won't have to review secrets or confidential stuff in the interview. And it helps to say a lot that this is research, nonprofit research. And so on the topic of the video, i find that in this level it's rather something to be treated more carefully. It's not the same incentive as it is for smaller initiatives. And that's it. And I went for a first one hour interview that she gave me in which she basically only spoke summarizing the whole sustainability strategy which is already defined in annual reports they have in their website that make I don't know how many pages like, fifty pages, something like that. It's a whole book every year, and that's it. And then I wrote to her again, saying that Sadie and I would be in Colombia and that we would really like to interview them again. And then it was amazing because we were there for one full day. And then half a day more to visit one of the initiatives we will speak about in a moment. And we practically had most of the sustainability team at different times in the room with us. So they dedicated like a full day of their team. Basically we extracted seven hours of recordings and plus the visit. And they were very open, which was very impressive to see. And with that maybe we can start speaking about the case specifically. So the sustainability area or strategy at Postobón is an area that is divided a little bit in two parts. One part is the director of sustainability, which they told us is sea level. So she speaks directly with the president of the company and so on. And then they have a foundation, and the foundation is funded and was created. And they sit right next to each other in the office. But legally it's a different group or institution than post one which brings them some tax advantages that we discussed in the interview as well. And then there is director of the foundation and the personnel that works on the two initiatives that they describe to us work for that for the foundation. The sustainability strategy they seem to have is very broad, it has many topics, but we focused on two main projects or programs they have. One of them is Heat Social, named after their line of juices heat in which they provide support to fruit producers in different ways that we can discuss. And the other one is called, I forget the name, programa Faro. And it's about supporting recyclers organizations, among other things. So that the recyclers organizations certify some level of recycling for posto one which is required by law in Colombia. So we were able to see a little bit something that has to do with providers and something that has to do with waste or what happens after, or something like that.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: I think to provide some context. So Ostoborn makes mostly drinks. ¿And so they need fruits, right? ¿And then so their product is water plus fruit, plus the bottle, plastic bottles, right? And so by law in Colombia, you're supposed to recycle the equivalent of forty percent of the material that you put in the bottles of the market. Stop me, Paulus, if I say something stupid. And so their problem is to get the right amount of fruit, for example, blackberries, mangoes, to create their drinks. And they want the appropriate level of quality and the appropriate quantity. And then they also need to demonstrate that they recycle forty percent of the amount of plastic that they have put on the market. The way this is done is by providing the state certificates that are given by the industrial recyclers. Those who take the bottles or the plastic from the bottles. They use bottles and they melt it and to make new plastic basically. ¿Okay? And so the circuit for the recycling is that the consumer drinks the bottle, then he puts it somewhere. And then the bottle has to be collected by recyclers. And these recyclers bring them directly or not to these big companies. But of course you will not have individual recyclers who bring this thing directly to the big company. So they are organized in small associations or larger associations where many recyclers come together use shared equipment, for example, compactors for the cardboard, grinders for the plastic. And they solder different types of plastic. And then they bring that to the recyclers who pay for the plastic by the weight. Depending on the type of plastic, it's a different price. And also they are paid by the municipality for the service of collecting the salted garbage. All right. ¿So the business model of the recyclers is they collect the plastic from the households, right? Then they bring it to the to the industrial recyclers. And they get money by the weight from the industrial recyclers. They get money from the municipality for doing the service. And in exchange of that, they get certificates of how much weight of plastic they have brought to the recyclers. And then Poster Bon needs to get these certificates to show that they comply with the regulation. And there are two types of ways of getting these certificates. Either you could buy them from the recyclers, which is what Coca Cola does. Or you can have the recyclers give them to you in exchange of something. Which is what Postal Bond does. And Postal Bond has organized in such a way that they help the associations. So that the associations in return are happy to give them the proof. Always help me the recipes. The certificates they have taken from the industrial recycles. That's how the business looks downstream. And at the moment, apparently they have more than one hundred percent, which is far above what is required. ¿Okay? But they don't give money to the recyclers to get the certificates, as I understand. And now, if you at the upstream model, the problem is to get the fruit from the producers. And it is not simple in Colombia. It's not simple for several reasons. ¿The first thing is you might have a problem for the crop, right? Is that it might be too dry if there's not enough water then you got no fruit or then you can have the fruit and it's bad quality. Maybe because the guys, the producers, they don't really know how to do these things or to process them correctly or to put them correctly in the truck. Then you have the transport issues, the road system is very bad, so the fruit can become damaged during transport. If that is the case, this is a net loss for the producer because they cannot go back with their truck and with their damaged stuff. ¿Right? And so Postobun can't use it and the producer can't use it. So the interest of Postobn is that these guys manage to have a crop, that they collect it properly, that it's the right type of stuff and the right quantity that Postobon needs. And that it arrives in good shape at the place of transformation where they do their your whole business. And so what Postobon does with these guys is that they set up a series of contracts which takes a bit of time to have a long term contract on how much type of which fruit they will get a minimum amount. And then they fix a price, a fixed price in advance, which is good for the producer because men problem of the producer is precisely that they don't have any anticipation of how much they will sell. So it gives them a guarantee which also enables them to invest if necessary, because they know that they will be able to get the right price for this specific quantity. I forgot if Postobun actually pays them beforehand with a minimum or only on delivery. This is something I don't remember probably somewhere in the interview. And Postopon has created very, very detailed accountings of all these agreements with the local producers who themselves are in associations. And the thing that they have to do with the producers is not just about the quantity and the quality, but also of the modalities of delivery. And they also have to make sure that these guys have a proper accounting system which is very often not the case. So they want to have these people providing what they want in terms of products and also in terms of information, because they will need this for traceability. Also because they want to have some stuff that's organic, for example. Then they want to maybe be able to prove where it comes, from how, etcetera. So they have all these things in large Excel spreadsheets and they have the documents and the contracts and apparently takes quite a bit of time to establish a contract because they get to know the people, they check everything and after some point in discussion and forgot how long it takes. These guys finally have this contract. And interestingly, what they told us, and I was very surprised, is that even though they have this contract and say, okay, you give us like, forty tons of blackberries or whatever, we get that price now, for example, if there's no rain and the guy cannot deliver the forty tons. Well, I mean, there's not real a penalty or whatever they just discuss, it's much more humane than we would expect with what's written in the contract. If there's a problem, they just try to see how to solve it. Okay. And maybe next year they will compensate or something like this. But they seem to have an understanding. And what's written in the contract is more of an objective than something that they really have to comply with. Although Postobón seems to fulfill their obligations, but it doesn't seem that the producers, when they don't fulfill the obligations, they get a severe penalty about the contract. That was my understanding. ¿Is that correct?

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Yes, that's correct. I got the impression that the quantity and all that was more to benefit the producer than Postobón to give some insurance to the producer. Yes, that's right. Something we saw and we started to understand, and was very surprising to us, is that in both cases, what you see is that the company, in order to achieve the same goals of securing the provision of fruits or securing plastic that is recuperated, has different alternatives to achieve this. And some companies would only pay for both things and look for different providers. And things like that others might try to buy the whole chain. They have chosen a way that is responsible with the social system or the social groups that are around the company. Because they see themselves as an actor in that. And they do it by supporting small producers or associations. And so on that are vulnerable and it costs them in money. And they know how much they do detailed calculations every time of how much it costs them. And how much that increases the price of the fruit they are paying, for example. But they see it as an insurance policy, as a thing to. Yes, we said many times something like that. If something happens, anything climatic, social violence, whatever we know, we will still have fruit and plastic because of this.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: So they invest in social capital. And this is understood as insurance against risk. And they talk about risk. And they seem to have experience of what is risk. So the feeling we have is that because they have experience of what chaos is, they're actually ready to pay for the extra cost. For example, they pay for the insurance of transport for the fruit. It is not. They pay for that even though if the fruit is damaged, it's the producer that gets the money. ¿Why is that? Suppose that a small producer loses their whole crop, then the guy's bankrupt and then they lose a producer. And considering the transaction costs and the investments that they have made with these guys to make sure they get the right quality that they know, the right person that they have trained into their accounting system and blah, blah, blah. This isn't worth it. That's how they justify that we didn't have the feeling that they have an economic calculation of all this. In terms of strategy, they know very well how much it costs them. ¿And it's about ten percent more in the price of buying the fruit, right? ¿Which seems acceptable because these guys seem to make about ten percent growth per year, right? I mean postable and. ¿But the way they presented it at least in the foundation, right? Was not economic calculation and risk. This is what we're interpreting as Western investigators. But what they say is more that they want to help the providers make. Sure, everything's going fine work for the development of the country and not in a nationalistic way. But it's like the understanding that if the whole ecosystem with whom they work operates well, it will benefit them in the end. But even though they are requested and they do have a very detailed accounting system, they showed us the Excel spreadsheet. I wouldn't say that this is piloting their action. This is not Excel table management. It's just that they need to keep trace of everything they're doing. But they do not use this as far as we've seen to make specific decisions. Because even if the guys fail, they stay with them. It's more of a system of making sure that they have understanding of what goes on and that they know the prices and that they know the cost and that it's going to help them and that they have a better discussion also on price and everything. But they make sure that the guys can live. ¿You see what I mean? So I think the calculations also help them to do this. It's like they sit with these guys and try to figure out what they can do and then they make that they put that somewhere in the contract. My understanding is that a lot of these guys don't have a very high level of qualification. I mean, the previous producers. ¿That is certainly the case for the recyclers, right? We were not shocked, but I mean, these guys, the Recyclers who we saw, they're very poor. Like you know, before these associations, I'm sure that these guys were, many of them were sleeping in the street. And so the creation of this Association of Recyclers has a very strong social impact. And interestingly they are trying to scale that up at national level. And they're helping these guys to organize. And they say that they have seventy percent of the Recyclers in the program or something like that.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Ten percent of all the Recyclers in the country work for them or through them in the associations they support. It's like six thousand. Something like that. Yes.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: And so they help these associations to operate. And as an example, we talk about this when we talk about coprofair call in the next replay interview. But Sofia witnessed while we were visiting there in the margins, two negotiations. So we went there with an anthropologist from the Foundation. And the other guy, ¿Is he from the Foundation or is he from the? ¿What was his name?

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: He's from the Foundation as well. Yes.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: So maybe you can tell them about, you know, the Conveyor Belt and the Solar Panels.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Yes. We were able to see one directly, one of the transactions they do with these types of Association. And it was very impressive because it's not about money at all. It's not about I will give you money for the certificate or whatever. It's about this type of support we've been discussing. So in the middle of the visit we were doing, the person from the Foundation tells the director or creator of the association. OK. As we had discussed, we found a Conveyor Belt that might work for you. And the company is happy to not use it anymore or is no longer using. Has been put apart for you to take a look at it and make sure that it works for you. So we will give that to you. We are happy to give that to you. It will be assembled and brought here to the Association. So you will have it for. Yeah, he didn't say that. But you will have it for free. And this corresponds to this many tons of. So if you agree, this would correspond to these many tons of plastic that you would certify for postal over. I don't know how many months. A few months and so on. So they have a precise calculation of how much that plastic costs versus the Conveyor Belt and so on, because it was a specific number. But the other is happy to do it because they will sell that plastic and get paid for it through the other sources. So it's a win win. Postovon supports the organization, the organization supports Postovon and they don't exchange money. And then when we were leaving in the visit, the person from the association says, oh, you know, I have been thinking about installing solar panels because we pay something like a thousand or two thousand euros per month in electricity with all these machines. And I found a deal where I could still pay the same per month and then have the solar panels financed for that amount of and install them and so on. So could posto one support part of that and then the other says, ah, that's interesting. He asks some more details and then he says, I will find out and we will be discussing that. So there are definitely transactions in some way and negotiations, but they are of a very special kind because it's. It's support slash money, but not. Yeah, like resources versus the plastic and so on.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: And it's informal, Right. So this actually evades tax. And also I understand that they gave them some vehicles. No.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Also another support they gave was vehicles for some motorcycles for the recyclers to collect the material and the change it's in the transcripts, but the change in in productivity was amazing like the amount of recycled materials that a person can collect in one day with these manual cards, they use gets multiplied by. It was not even ten times. It was more when they get a motorcycle that has been put into something they can use for that. So Postoone also provides or they get a similar agreement for the motorcycles and then the productivity of the whole thing is multiplied many times because of that.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: So so our. We went into detail about what kind of transactions they have with the various stakeholders and it turns out that there's not so much with the government, but they do still have things with the governments and with the municipalities and for example for buildings and things like that. ¿And was it them who made that? ¿School powders?

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: No, that's crepes. That's another one.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: But they.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Do support. They give tables and chairs which they make from recycled plastic from their bottle.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: It's not plastic. It's actually when you have a tetra pack, you're right. And what they do is they compress these things and they make chairs and tables which they give to the associations. And we saw them when we were there with the recyclers. ¿But they also give these two schools because they want to have the children of their farmers educated because they say, we want them to stay local, right? And so we want them to be trained and we want these, you know, they want to improve their local life conditions, these guys. And then another aspect of this is that if there are schools, then the parents actually can work. So this increases the productivity. Otherwise if they have to keep the kids at home, then they will not be able to work as much. So it's beneficial for everyone. And so they have good relationships with the schools because they provide them with the tables and they. They showed us pictures with all the kids having these little tables and so that's in a way. That's a way for them to recycle this thing. They wouldn't know what to do with the tetra pack stuff. And this becomes something that actually helps their providers in many ways, not just having the kids being taught, but also having the kids being looked after. And so the parents are better off. And I think they said to us something about the fact that in terms of sustainability, I mean, the kids can actually teach the parents. I don't remember exactly what that was. We'd have to look it up, but I felt that they organized sessions of training for the whole families and where they were explaining them how to do whatever I forgot it somewhere in the transcript. And so that was for the kids and for the parents at the same time, but. But apparently the kids that would resonate with what they were taught at school or something like this, because the program of giving the tables, I think is not just giving the tables. I think it also comes with some educational stuff that they do with the schools. ¿Paul, you somebody reinventing?

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: No, no, I think that's right. I didn't understood if they were already doing it or they were planning, but is definitely something. And similarly they also have a program to promote physical activity in schools and one in which they give bicycles to families, especially for kids to go to school because otherwise they have to walk for two or three hours to get there and so on. And these programs that we just mentioned, I got the impression that I think they said in one moment that they were less like, yeah they were less important or they wanted to scale them down or something like that. They said because, and this was a big word they use many times they wanted shared value initiatives and they do see this as shared value. But I got the impression. The other two in that sense were.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: I think, Paulus, I think it's because now all they all have bicycles.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Okay.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: That was one reason. Is that, you know, you do this once the equipment. And now this. I think that the kids already had these bicycles. So they were. ¿What was it? ¿Haibikes or whatever the name? The same problem. I forgot the name. It's the same name as their program. Hit Bikes. No, something like that, Maybe.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Yes. One interesting thing I remembered is, they said that how. ¿Because we asked how do you choose the school that will get this support? And they said we called the managers of our plant and we asked them to give us the name of the schools that are around the plant. Which shows that it's not like any school in Colombia in any area. It's the people that are likely to work in the plant as well.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: And so on, they were very clear. They're not a charity. This is not just to help people. This is actually to out of pure benevolence. This is to support the people they are working with. So they are improving their, I would say, their nutrients, their feeding system. You see upwards and upstream and downstream. They are side by side with these guys trying to figure out what they want, what they need, so that they can upgrade in a way the quality, the competences and all that. This is typically what we here would do with divisions of our own organization and perhaps sometimes with some of our subcontractors. But with the subcontractors, we'd rather set norms. ¿And they would have to do this, right? But there it's not that. It's actually, they help the people to get the competences, get the equipment, get the affordances to do these things. And they, you know, they train them and they have been careful in having people who are able to discuss with them. So like this anthropologist who is actually speaking with them. And it was interesting because when we went there, the guy had something in the trunk he had brought for these guys. So they create relationships that go beyond business. There's trust here. So what they are building, there is really, they're building trust and they're building long term relationships. And it was clear that in case I think they said it explicitly, if or when we have a problem, we will be able to trust these guys and they will be in together. This is not a competitive market. They are not operating as homo economicus. They are operating as members with different roles of one given community. That is feeding the food chain. It's still the food chain. There's nothing as a charity or we work with these guys. We want them to be in the good condition to provide what we need. It's a bit more than to provide us what we need. It's more. We want the whole chain to be resilient and efficient. They are. And I don't remember if it's these ones or lucr, they make sure they train their providers to have more clients than themselves.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: And produce more products than what they sell to the company because.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: They don't want them to become dependent.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: I think that's very interesting because you mentioned the same for crepes.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: With the.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: With the people in Montez de Maria and the bean seeds, they're trying to actually diversify their crops and make sure that they're not fully dependent on crepes. And it's very interesting to see this like trust relationships that these big companies want to build. But I don't know also if you were able to sense how do they navigate the tensions between all of these like sustainability and social goals versus the fact that they're still like a profit driven business and they have a set of objectives. I know you talked with the people in the foundation, but I don't know.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: If this also has to do with.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: The tax benefits of the programs and the fact that they are a foundation itself which gives them lots of benefits.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: We asked them that the tax stuff there is a possibility to do this without being a foundation because you have a law in Colombia that enables you to invest your benefits into something like this instead of tax. ¿We asked the question to some others Why do you do a foundation? Blah, blah. And so they could actually do things without this being a foundation. I forgot why they said it was a foundation what go ahead.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Because they. Because they get. ¿What's it? A twenty five percent rebate in taxes from the things they invest.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: That's right. ¿But suppose they would use immediately their benefits? They would get one hundred percent.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Yeah, but this is what I wanted to say. I don't see, they don't describe it as a tension between the business and the sustainability which you would think of if you think only in terms of money, they see it as a long term vision. So it might mean you will get less money now in the short term. But it will mean that you will still be able to produce money In ten years. I think we discussed something on those lines and they calculate how much more they are paying for the fruit because they have those programs, for example. And they are happy to pay a little bit more because they know the chain is resilient.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: Something very important is that these are still family owned businesses and so the director of the foundation, Katerina.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: ¿Is that right, Marta? And I know Marta is the director of sustainability, Catalina.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: Yes. And so she said, and Paulius said, you know that whenever she comes and goes and she's the boss and says, i want this, I want that for sustainability. Basically the answer is always yes. And so this does not seem to be the way they calculate. I would say that the best comparison will be, you know, these Catholic entrepreneurs and capitalists of the nineteenth century that were building villages and schools and hospitals for their workforce and giving them small gardens so they would have fresh vegetables, etcetera. That's exactly it. These guys are doing good, but in such a way that it also brings them benefits. So they get the moral satisfaction, they get a national satisfaction. ¿And I'm sure they're seen by municipalities and other stakeholders because they replace the state, okay? ¿They do stuff for the people, so they get this goodwill from everyone because they're useful, they're an employer and benefits, and they help, right? ¿And then it is still profitable, right? And that's the proof and the pudding. But I think that the rationale is, you know, as I said, we look for the long term, this is a risky market, This is a risky situation. It's exactly like an insurance. If I get a long term relationship, it will be beneficial. If it's the short term, the first big problem, and we know it will happen, will wipe us out. So in a way, the way I interpreted, but that was not something that they actually told us, and I tried to push them in that direction, but that didn't really come, you know, in the car when we were coming back, but she wasn't really responded to that, Catalina, it was like, oh, you have experienced chaos and now you're back. And in a way, this is why you're so sensitive to long term and risk and trust and stuff like that. ¿But it didn't seem that they were thinking it that way, right? Even though they express it. ¿I would, but that's my personal feeling, right? Say that it is for them normal to have good relationships with their providers and that these relationship go beyond the current business and are more generic we're in business and I'm interested. Just I would. I don't know. Just I would ask my colleagues or my employees, whatever, I'm trying to know if everything's okay, any problems at home, anything. ¿Right? Because, you know, it's a mixture of this paternalistic approach. Like, I want to make sure that I have good workforce. And if they're not in good state, this is going to be bad for the business. But it's more than that. It's. I am sincere when I do this because I'm happy to have good relationships. And I'm not just, you know, the boss. I'm a human being and I'm really happy if the guys are happy. And I felt that when they were talking about their relationships with these guys, and this is also what I think I felt when we went on the field with them, there is something genuine. They were not faking it. They're not coming there to look at the bottom line of the whatever. And then, how much you give me certificates, they come. ¿And then they try to figure out, okay, how is it going the business? ¿Right? ¿How are you guys doing? And then they talk about other things. And then they try to understand in a holistic way the situation of these people on the ground. ¿And if they see there are problems that they can help solving whether it brings them profit or not, doesn't matter because they were managing the relationship, Right? And the relationship is a very important asset. And I think this is why my interpretation is, they've been to chaos and back. And they know that if you collaborate with people, then you'll manage to solve the situation. If you have an antagonistic relationship with them or just, you know, strategic, instrumental, transactional, then you're. It will not work. People will not get out of their way to help you if you're not getting out of your way to help them now. ¿Right? And they're in power position, but the day there's a problem. They will need to have the collective intelligence and help. And they're nurturing this. They are nurturing their environments beyond their own immediate interest. And in doing this, they are not homo economicus. We are seeing people who seem to be full human beings and not just homo economicus. There is something more in the relation that they have on the field that we have seen, or even together, that they do not act only as the instantaneous self utility maximizing economic actor. There is more than that. And in this perspective, they have a systemic view. They have a larger view than the economic transaction, and that's sustainability. As we see, it long term understanding of the health of the whole system. And the health is not just economic. It's technical and it's social. And they take that into account. But they do it not like we do it here by the book, like we look. Okay. ¿What's the pillar number one, pillar number three? Pillar number three. That's not how it happens. These guys are trying to see from the perspective of their stakeholders what problem they have sincerely. And they try to see if they can solve it with what they have. And if they can they do. Of course, they still are aware of their own limits. And that at the end of the year, they will have to justify that they have not wasted much money and that everything works, etcetera. But if they can't do more, they will do more. And they will not be expecting to have something in return because they've given more. This is just my projection. I don't know. But I felt it like this for us. Who are used to discussing with people in the West and who are Business, you know, that is not the way they operate. This is not business, as we say it. ¿This is activity, right?

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Yes. And this is even more impressive when you consider the context in Colombia and the levels of distrust of people, especially in more vulnerable groups towards the state and towards big company. It's really impressive that they have managed to. To bridge those things and be able to collaborate in those ways with them. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: No. ¿You wanted to say something else?

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: I was thinking two things that might be interesting to follow up. Because I know you will have. Yes, two things. And I was looking a little bit at the history of the company. I don't know if and how we could get something in those lines, but I feel there's something there. Because in the interviews the people were young and the others had joined the company no more than ten years ago. And so on. So we got a vision of the last few years. But I was looking that this company has been around for one hundred twenty years. So they have suffered all the violence waves and then narco traffic, and then whatever else. And they have become the it's the largest drink company in the country and one of the largest of South America. And trying to understand a little bit this and this resilience or chaos and back and so on, would be very interesting. At least a little bit, or at least how they. How they see today. Because there is a sense of this chaos today or the possibility of this chaos, which is not that they are feeling it right now. It's rather something that could happen again, but rest. Trying to understand that a little bit. And the other thing which relates to the message from Paulo sent in the chat is the relationship of these with the rest of the conglomerate. They are a part of. So it's not only the twelve companies, but the person that owns this is an organization with his name. And it's one of the richest persons in the country. They have eighty companies and more interestingly, maybe they have one of the largest sugar producing companies and one of the largest radio and television groups as well. So this would be interesting as well.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: It would be really interesting to understand when they had sort of the insight of why it's worse to do this. We had this discussion this morning with Helga. And where we're coming to is, you know, to behave like this. You need to have the belief that this is worth it and that you actually can do it. So you have the efficacy that if we do a pray like this, it's still going to be okay and profitable. But seeing before that, I mean, this sounds crazy if you just think, in terms of business, in the classic economic approach, you would never do this. You would just do the minimum and try to challenge various producers to get the best price exactly what they're doing in the west. So there must have been some experience that made them change their ways. I think it's absolutely crucial that these guys are a family owned business. The three that we saw that had the same strategy. I mean, Luker, Waffle, Krebs and this one. They are all family run business, even though they're big. And the influence of the founder is very important. And the importance of people, it was very clear that smarta the director for sustainability. This is a strong personality. And she was very, very important in all the creation of this. So she must have the trust of the boss somewhere. It's not easy. And obviously, she's doing a lot of work and she was instrumental. So she's wow, she's a woman that, you know, she is good and smart and. And works hard. Okay.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Yeah.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: And so if she wasn't there, that wouldn't have happened. But probably also the boss of the company, the owner, whatever his name is, done his name here. Miguel Fernando Escobar.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Yeah. And, yeah, they did say in one moment that Marta, when she arrived, had put together all this strategy in this way. No, but we didn't get much information of what happened before and how was the strategy there.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: Yeah, You see, one thing that happened is that in the beginning, martyr was with us. Right. And so they had not planned that we would stay seven hours. Right. They probably thought the beginning, the morning And the idea was, Paulius is going to tell us the usual shebang about sustainability, blah, blah, blah, etc. And then she would leave us with the foundation. And then we get, you know, they would give us some bottles of poster burn. And then tote bag and everything would be fine. And after she had talked for fifteen minutes. I was very impolite. I stopped her presentation. Say, you know, this is not what we want. I'm sorry. We want precise fact, why you're doing this, etcetera, etc. And I think she was upset. And she was right to be so. And so I think she's still a bit upset. And that's why she left. But she would have left anyway. But this puts the discussion on a completely different track. Because they started to understand what we were after. Like, we're not journalists. And even though, I mean, they had seen us before, you know, we did not talk with them. As we are coming here to listen, it's like we're coming here to discuss. And so I'm afraid I broke a bit the relationship with Marta. ¿So next time, it's better be who goes, right? But in any case, we had a very good, very, very detailed discussion. With these guys. From the Sustainability Foundation. From the Foundation. And actually, they started giving us in the discussion. They shared their Excel spreadsheet. With the real figures and the names and all the rest of it. So this was not recorded, but were here. And then, well, the talk was recorded. So this is really typically not the kind of stuff. ¿That they perhaps should have shared, right? Because they trusted us. But, you know, because we asked very, very specific questions. And the guys were there and they just shared it. And so we started to understand That they actually have a very, very precise idea. Of how much this cost. So this is not like they're naive and giving away money. They know what they're doing. And they are aware of how much it costs. And they go anyway. And so what we would like to do with Palius when we go back there is. But we need to have a conversation with them beforehand is saying, look, we love what you're doing. Guys, this is really good. And we believe that. I mean, I'm sincere when I say this. ¿I say, can we sit down together and have a look at the finances of that stuff? And then we write a paper together. And we show that it is worth it to play the sustainability game at least in Colombia. And we need their help, and not just their help. We need their in a way their commitment and their consent to do this. And we're not going to do this in a way that will not be detrimental to them. But they must trust us in the way we do this. And I think this is what we should have. Because I've never seen such figures. And I've never seen such figures of this company. That is actually sustainable. I mean, these guys play the game for real and they win. So. And I think it's exactly the same with Krebs and with Luker. And so it turns out these guys can do this because there's no pressure from their shareholders. ¿Every quarter say, how much money did you bring? ¿And a pension fund say, how come you pay ten percent more for the fruits than your competitors? But the proof is, in the pudding, these guys make ten percent growth on average. So that is what we have to figure out. ¿The big question is, is this also valid in less risky environments? This I don't know. Because what you do when you do investments in social capital is actually that you save transaction costs in the future. And that's exactly the nature of what an organization is, according to a Coase and Williamson. So an organization is a way to set up, you know, processes. So you do not have to haggle and discuss every time and you save transaction costs. And that's exactly what they're doing. They're discussing with them the quantity, the quality, the format, the dates and everything, just as if they were in the organization. Except that they do not, I mean, hold the risk of having them inside. So they have externalized part of the risk, but not everything. And here we externalize everything of the risk because we know there's not much risk. But they have a different approach. They share the risk in a way with their partners and they pay for that. The fact, I was flabbergasted to realize that these guys are paying the insurance for the crops. So. And it's interesting they told us this also. This is not them because they're in with the bank and also the insurance company. But the banks are apparently part of the deal somewhere in the way they have set up the insurance contracts. So we'd have to look at that more in detail. Exactly how all these contracts written. That would be fascinating to see. Just that as a case, how they share the risk in monetary terms and the results of that. Because I'd be interested to see. Okay, over ten years you guys set up that system. ¿How many times did you pay? ¿Was it worth it? ¿We should try to meet the guys who set up the insurance stuff and say, can we please have the figures?

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Yeah, that's very good. ¿Did I understand correctly that the bank is part of their group as well?

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: I don't know, Juan Pablo. That's what I was looking. They have a bank called Financiera. Is the only one, at least in Wikipedia. I don't know. It's probably not very big. Is the one that started as a part of which one of their was one of their largest companies on fabrics. But that I wasn't. ¿I was gonna push back a bit on that because I mean, with these guys, yeah, of course what they do is compelling, and it's great, but they're also as you were into, like as your intuition was taking you, they're heavily integrated vertically, horizontally and every way, right? ¿So, I mean, what happens not in this particular example, but what they do in construction, for example, is the guy owns everything, right? So he owns the constructing company, the bank, the pension fund, the roads, etcetera. ¿So something that seems great, it really, really like he's kind of hedging himself with his own money or with public money that he controls, right? ¿So, I mean, I don't know if this is the case, but what I'm saying is maybe my major pushback to particularly this company or this way of operating is how big do you need to be to actually have the possibility of operating like this, right? ¿They feel no market pressure, whatsoever, right? ¿Coca Cola is never going to win in Colombia, right? ¿They have absolute control of the market and basically of people's lives, right? ¿Because this is similar with Olympica in the Caribbean coast, you know, these guys own their supermarket, the radio station, the football team, right? ¿They control people's lives through their products, right? Because they're so big that they can do that. So I don't know if in a different context you can actually spend the way they do to kind of invest in their future, which is great, but maybe they don't behave like capitalists because they are not. They're basically, you know, Plutocrast, Plutocrist or whatever. I don't know how to say that, but they have absolute control of everything.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Yeah, we say they own the country basically, yeah, but you could do the opposite argument. You could say that because they own everything. They don't need to do this stuff and they still do it.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: ¿No, no, because they still need the raw material to operate, right? What they're doing is hedging themselves one hundred percent to always have control of the raw material so they can produce their soft drinks. Just a minute. I need to have a quick phone call.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Okay. Yes. One thing for the recording as well that I. I think is interesting to see in this case and others, is a little bit that the cost of all these compared to their size or operations, i mean, the sustainability thing apparently costs them. ¿How much is this? Around nine million dollars per year. ¿How much is that of their benefit per year? ¿Is it a very small part or a large part? And then we can get some idea of how important it is. Okay. Good. I don't know if along, like during the conversation there were any other cases that they flagged that could be interesting to look at. Yeah. We never went to fruit producer because all their fruit producers are in other regions. I'm sure they mentioned some. They mentioned some. We have already seen for other cases. Invima. Andy. ¿What else? The company that buys the recycled the plastic for recycling which apparently there's only one or two in the country. Might be interesting. ¿And what's it called? I don't know. Let me check.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: I do have a question when you're ready.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Yes. Anka. Okay. Yeah. Okay. Yeah. I was saying sad, apart from some other stuff that I think one of the things we could do is to check how big is the investment in sustainability they do because they invest around. ¿What was it? Nine million dollars per year in sustainability compared to the annual benefits of the company to see if it's a very small or not such a small part which would tell us a little bit about the the scale. And so on.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: We do have to see that. Yeah.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: ¿What was your question?

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: Sorry, I muted. ¿Mine is if you discuss by any chance the product itself, Right? Because soft drinks.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Yes.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: Soft drinks are not super sustainable in terms of public health and things like that. Right. So I don't know how much of that and heat and all of these are. They're recognized as very horrible products for mass consumption because of the very high sugar, etcetera. So I don't know if there's an internal reflection of that.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Or the recent there is a little bit. And that's an interesting topic because there is a little bit. They say that they are trying to reduce the sugar in their drinks and that they have a goal for that reducing it in some percentage. entries.text: Okay, so I don't know which of you would like to start telling me about the overall context of Postovon and how you managed to contact them and get the interview wonderful. Yes, I can start describing that so Postobón is one of the largest companies in Colombia. They have been around for a few decades already. I also read somewhere that they were one of the biggest companies in Latin America as well, they started producing soft carbonated drinks so sodas. And this is still the main part of their business. Although they have started to produce also juices and even condiments and sauces and other things. They explained to us that what we call Postobón is actually a group of twelve companies and it includes many things. It includes a transport company, it includes a company that does reforestation. It includes several things. The way we contacted the Initiative was through LinkedIn. I reached out to the sustainability director. I didn't know her, but it turns out we had some connections in common because she was the environment secretary of Sergio Fajardo, the former mayor of Medellín, and I worked with Antanas Motzkus. And they worked together. So we had several people in common and she took a while. But she was very enthusiastic to answer and to agree to an interview still maybe in methodological terms. I think I learned with this case that these meso cases have to be treated a little bit different than the smaller cases that we have done. You have to be much more careful. They are still interested on being interviewed by European Institute and this is a major motivation. But they also need some reassurance that you are not going to sell their data or to use it for whatever purpose. That they won't have to review secrets or confidential stuff in the interview. And it helps to say a lot that this is research, nonprofit research. And so, on the topic of the video, i find that in this level it's rather something to be treated more carefully. It's not the same incentive as it is for smaller initiatives. And that's it. And I went for a first one hour interview that she gave me in which she basically only spoke summarizing the whole sustainability strategy which is already defined in annual reports they have in their website that make I don't know how many pages like, fifty pages, something like that. It's a whole book every year, and that's it. And then I wrote to her again, saying that Sadie and I would be in Colombia and that we would really like to interview them again. And then it was amazing because we were there for one full day. And then half a day more to visit one of the initiatives we will speak about in a moment. And we practically had most of the sustainability team at different times in the room with us. So they dedicated like a full day of their team. Basically we extracted seven hours of recordings and plus the visit. And they were very open, which was very impressive to see. And with that maybe we can start speaking about the case specifically. So the sustainability area or strategy at Postobón is an area that is divided a little bit in two parts. One part is the director of sustainability, which they told us is sea level. So she speaks directly with the president of the company and so on. And then they have a foundation, and the foundation is funded and was created. And they sit right next to each other in the office. But legally it's a different group or institution than post one which brings them some tax advantages that we discussed in the interview as well. And then there is director of the foundation and the personnel that works on the two initiatives that they describe to us work for that for the foundation. The sustainability strategy they seem to have is very broad, it has many topics, but we focused on two main projects or programs they have. One of them is Heat Social, named after their line of juices heat in which they provide support to fruit producers in different ways that we can discuss. And the other one is called, I forget the name, programa Faro. And it's about supporting recyclers organizations, among other things. So that the recyclers organizations certify some level of recycling for posto one which is required by law in Colombia. So we were able to see a little bit something that has to do with providers and something that has to do with waste or what happens after, or something like that.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: I think to provide some context. So Ostoborn makes mostly drinks. ¿And so they need fruits, right? ¿And then so their product is water plus fruit, plus the bottle, plastic bottles, right? And so by law in Colombia, you're supposed to recycle the equivalent of forty percent of the material that you put in the bottles of the market. Stop me, Paulus, if I say something stupid. And so their problem is to get the right amount of fruit, for example, blackberries, mangoes, to create their drinks. And they want the appropriate level of quality and the appropriate quantity. And then they also need to demonstrate that they recycle forty percent of the amount of plastic that they have put on the market. The way this is done is by providing the state certificates that are given by the industrial recyclers. Those who take the bottles or the plastic from the bottles. They use bottles and they melt it and to make new plastic basically. ¿Okay? And so the circuit for the recycling is that the consumer drinks the bottle, then he puts it somewhere. And then the bottle has to be collected by recyclers. And these recyclers bring them directly or not to these big companies. But of course you will not have individual recyclers who bring this thing directly to the big company. So they are organized in small associations or larger associations where many recyclers come together use shared equipment, for example, compactors for the cardboard, grinders for the plastic. And they solder different types of plastic. And then they bring that to the recyclers who pay for the plastic by the weight. Depending on the type of plastic, it's a different price. And also they are paid by the municipality for the service of collecting the salted garbage. All right. ¿So the business model of the recyclers is they collect the plastic from the households, right? Then they bring it to the to the industrial recyclers. And they get money by the weight from the industrial recyclers. They get money from the municipality for doing the service. And in exchange of that, they get certificates of how much weight of plastic they have brought to the recyclers. And then Poster Bon needs to get these certificates to show that they comply with the regulation. And there are two types of ways of getting these certificates. Either you could buy them from the recyclers, which is what Coca Cola does. Or you can have the recyclers give them to you in exchange of something. Which is what Postal Bond does. And Postal Bond has organized in such a way that they help the associations. So that the associations in return are happy to give them the proof. Always help me the recipes. The certificates they have taken from the industrial recyclers. That's how the business looks downstream. And at the moment, apparently they have more than one hundred percent, which is far above what is required. ¿Okay? But they don't give money to the recyclers to get the certificates, as I understand. And now, if you at the upstream model, the problem is to get the fruit from the producers. And it is not simple in Colombia. It's not simple for several reasons. ¿The first thing is you might have a problem for the crop, right? Is that it might be too dry if there's not enough water then you got no fruit or then you can have the fruit and it's bad quality. Maybe because the guys, the producers, they don't really know how to do these things or to process them correctly or to put them correctly in the truck. Then you have the transport issues, the road system is very bad. So the fruit can become damaged during transport. If that is the case, this is a net loss for the producer because they cannot go back with their truck and with their damaged stuff. ¿Right? And so Postobun can't use it and the producer can't use it. So the interest of Postobn is that these guys manage to have a crop, that they collect it properly, that it's the right type of stuff and the right quantity that Postobon needs. And that it arrives in good shape at the place of transformation where they do their your whole business. And so what Postobon does with these guys is that they set up a series of contracts which takes a bit of time to have a long term contract on how much type of which fruit they will get a minimum amount. And then they fix a price, a fixed price in advance, which is good for the producer because men problem of the producer is precisely that they don't have any anticipation of how much they will sell. So it gives them a guarantee which also enables them to invest if necessary, because they know that they will be able to get the right price for this specific quantity. I forgot if Postobn actually pays them beforehand with a minimum or only on delivery. This is something I don't remember, probably somewhere in the interview. And Postopon has created very, very detailed accountings of all these agreements with the local producers who themselves are in associations. And the thing that they have to do with the producers is not just about the quantity and the quality, but also of the modalities of delivery. And they also have to make sure that these guys have a proper accounting system which is very often not the case. So they want to have these people providing what they want in terms of products and also in terms of information, because they will need this for traceability. Also because they want to have some stuff that's organic, for example. Then they want to maybe be able to prove where it comes, from how, etcetera. So they have all these things in large Excel spreadsheets and they have the documents and the contracts and apparently takes quite a bit of time to establish a contract because they get to know the people, they check everything and after some point in discussion and forgot how long it takes. These guys finally have this contract. And interestingly, what they told us, and I was very surprised, is that even though they have this contract and say, okay, you give us like, forty tons of blackberries or whatever, we get that price now, for example, if there's no rain and the guy cannot deliver the forty tons. Well, I mean, there's not real a penalty or whatever they just discuss, it's much more humane than we would expect with what's written in the contract. If there's a problem, they just try to see how to solve it. Okay. And maybe next year they will compensate or something like this. But they seem to have an understanding. And what's written in the contract is more of an objective than something that they really have to comply with. Although Postobón seems to fulfill their obligations, but it doesn't seem that the producers, when they don't fulfill the obligations, they get a severe penalty without the contract. That was my understanding polius. ¿Is that correct?

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: Yes, that's correct. I got the impression that the quantity and all that was more to benefit the producer than to give some insurance to the producer. Yes, that's right. Something we saw and we started to understand, and was very surprising to us, is that in both cases, what you see is that the company, in order to achieve the same goals of securing the provision of fruits or securing plastic that is recuperated, has different alternatives to achieve this. And some companies would only pay for both things and look for different providers and things like that others might try to buy the whole chain. They have chosen a way that is responsible with the social system or the social groups that are around the company. Because they see themselves as an actor in that. And they know how much they do detailed calculations every time of how much it costs them and how much that increases the price of the fruit they are paying, for example. But they make sure that the guys can live. ¿You see what I mean? So I think the calculations also help them to do this. Has been put apart for you to take a look at it and make sure that it works for you. So we will give that to you. We are happy to give that to you. It will be assembled and brought here to the Association. So you will have it for. Yeah, he didn't say that, but you will have it for free. And this corresponds to this many tons of. So if you agree, this would correspond to these many tons of plastic that you would certify for postal over. I don't know how many months. A few months and so on. So they have a precise calculation of how much that plastic costs versus the Conveyor Belt and so on, because it was a specific number. But the other is happy to do it because they will sell that plastic and get paid for it through the other sources. So it's a win win. Postovon supports the organization, the organization supports Postovon and they don't exchange money. And then when we were leaving in the visit, the person from the association says, oh, you know, I have been thinking about installing solar panel because we pay something like a thousand or two thousand euros per month in electricity with all these machines and I found a deal where I could still pay the same per month and then have the solar panels financed for that amount of and install them and so on. So could Postobon support part of that and then the other says, ah, that's interesting. He asks some more details and then he says, I will find out and we will be discussing that. So there are definitely transactions in some way and negotiations, but they are of a very special kind because it's. It's support slash money but not. Yeah, like resources versus the plastic and so on.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: And it's informal, Right. So this actually evades tax. And also I understand that they gave them some vehicles. No.

entries.speaker: Sofia

It was more when they get a motorcycle that has been put into something they can use for that. So Postoone also provides or they get a similar agreement for the motorcycles and then the productivity of the whole thing is multiplied many times because of that.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: So so our. We went into detail about what kind of transactions they have with the various stakeholders and it turns out that there's not so much with the government, but they do still have things with the governments and with the municipalities and for example for buildings and things like that. ¿And was it them who made that? ¿School powders?

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: No, that's crepes. That's another one.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: But they.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: Do support. They give tables and chairs which they make from recycled plastic from their bottle.

entries.speaker: Paulius

And what they do is they compress these things and they make chairs and tables which they give to the associations. And we saw them when we were there with the recyclers. ¿But they also give these two schools because they want to have the children of their farmers educated because they say we want them to stay local, right? And so we want them to be trained and we want these, you know, they want to improve their local life conditions, these guys. And then another aspect of this is that if there are schools, then the parents actually can work. So this increases the productivity. Otherwise if they have to keep the kids at home, then they will not be able to work as much. So it's beneficial for everyone. And so they have good relationships with the schools because they provide them with the tables and they. They showed us pictures with all the kids having these little tables and so that's in a way. That's a way for them to recycle this thing. They wouldn't know what to do with the tetra pack stuff. And this becomes something that actually helps their providers in many ways. Not just having the kids being taught, but also having the kids being looked after. And so the parents are better off. And I think they said to us something about the fact that in terms of sustainability, I mean, the kids can actually teach the parents. I don't remember exactly what that was. We'd have to look it up, but I felt that they organized sessions of training for the whole families and where they were explaining them how to do whatever. I forgot it somewhere in the transcript. And so that was for the kids and for the parents at the same time. But. But apparently the kids that would resonate with what they were taught at school or something like this. Because the program of giving the tables, I think is not just giving the tables. I think it also comes with some educational stuff that they do with the schools. ¿Paul, you somebody reinventing?

And similarly they also have a program to promote physical activity in schools and one in which they give bicycles to families, especially for kids to go to school because otherwise they have to walk for two or three hours to get there and so on. And these programs that we just mentioned, I got the impression that I think they said in one moment that they were less like Yeah, they were less important or they wanted to scale them down or something like that. They said because. And this was a big word they use many times. They wanted shared value initiatives and they do see this as shared value. But I got the impression the other two in that sense where I think.

Or whatever the name. The same problem. I forgot the name. It's the same name as their program. Hit Bikes. No, something like that, Maybe.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: Yes. One interesting thing I remembered is, they said that how. ¿Because we asked how do you choose the school that will get this support? And they said we called the managers of our plant and we asked them to give us the name of the schools that are around the plant. Which shows that it's not like any school in Colombia, in any area. It's the people that are likely to work in the plant as well.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: And so on, they were very clear. They're not a charity. This is not just to help people. This is actually out of pure benevolence. This is to support the people they are working with. So they are improving their, I would say, their nutrients, their feeding system. You see upwards and upstream and downstream. They are side by side with these guys trying to figure out what they want, what they need, so that they can upgrade in a way the quality, the competences and all that. This is typically what we here would do with divisions of our own organization and perhaps sometimes with some of our subcontractors. But with the subcontractors, we'd rather set norms. ¿And they would have to do this, right? But there it's not that. It's actually, they help the people to get the competences, get the equipment, get the affordances to do these things. And they, you know, they train them and they have been careful in having people who are able to discuss with them. So like this anthropologist who is actually speaking with them. And it was interesting because when we went there, the guy had something in the trunk he had brought for these guys. So they create relationships that go beyond business. There's trust here. So what they are building, there is really, they're building trust and they're building long term relationships. And it was clear that in case I think they said it explicitly, if or when we have a problem, we will be able to trust these guys and they will be in together. This is not a competitive market. They are not operating as homo economicus. They are operating as members with different roles of one given community. That is feeding the food chain. It's still the food chain. There's nothing as a charity or we work with these guys. We want them to be in the good condition to provide what we need. It's a bit more than to provide us what we need. It's more. We want the whole chain to be resilient and efficient. They are, and I don't remember if it's these ones or lucr, they make sure they train their providers to have more clients than themselves.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: And produce more products than what they sell to the.

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Company because they don't want them to become dependent.

With the people in Montez de Maria and the bean seeds, they're trying to actually diversify their crops and make sure that they're not fully dependent on crepes. And it's very interesting to see this like trust relationships that these big companies want to build. But I don't know also if you were able to sense how do they navigate the tensions between all of these like sustainability and social goals versus the fact that they're still like a profit driven business and they have a set of objectives. I know you talked with the people in the foundation, but I don't know if this also has to do with the tax benefits of the programs and the fact that they are a foundation itself which gives them lots of benefits.

entries.speaker: Sofia

So it might mean you will get less money now in the short term. But it will mean that you will still be able to produce money In ten years. I think we discussed something on those lines and they calculate how much more they are paying for the fruit because they have those programs, for example, and they are happy to pay a little bit more because they know the chain is resilient.

entries.speaker: Paulius

And so this does not seem to be the way they calculate. I would say that the best comparison will be, you know, these Catholic entrepreneurs and capitalists of the nineteenth century that were building villages and schools and hospitals for their workforce and giving them small gardens so they would have fresh vegetables, etcetera. That's exactly it. These guys are doing good, but in such a way that it also brings them benefits. So they get the moral satisfaction, they get a national satisfaction. ¿And I'm sure they're seen by municipalities and other stakeholders because they replace the state, okay? They do stuff for the people. ¿So they get this goodwill from everyone because they're useful, they're an employer and benefits, and they help, right? ¿And then it is still profitable, right? And that's the proof and the pudding. But I think that the rationale is, you know, as I said, we look for the long term, this is a risky market. This is a risky situation. It's exactly like an insurance. If I get a long term relationship, it will be beneficial. If it's the short term, the first big problem, and we know it will happen, will wipe us out. So in a way, the way I interpreted, but that was not something that they actually told us, and I tried to push them in that direction, but that didn't really come, you know, in the car when we were coming back, but she wasn't really responded to that. Catalina, it was like, oh, you have experienced chaos and now you're back. And in a way, this is why you're so sensitive to long term and risk and trust and stuff like that. ¿But it didn't seem that they were thinking it that way, right? Even though they express it. ¿I would, but that's my personal feeling, right? Say that it is for them normal to have good relationships with their providers and that these relationship go beyond the current business and are more generic we're in business and I'm interested. Just I would. I don't know. Just I would ask my colleagues or my employees, whatever, I'm trying to know if everything's okay, any problems at home, anything. ¿Right? Because, you know, it's a mixture of this paternalistic approach like I want to make sure that I have good workforce. And if they're not in good state, this is going to be bad for the business. But it's more than that. It's. I am sincere when I do this because I'm happy to have good relationships. And I'm not just, you know, the boss. I'm a human being and I'm really happy if the guys are happy. And I felt that when they were talking about their relationships with these guys, and this is also what I think I felt when we went on the field with them. There is something genuine. They were not faking it. They're not coming there to look at the bottom line of the whatever. ¿And then how much you give me certificates? ¿They come and then they try to figure out, okay, how is it going the business? ¿Right? ¿How are you guys doing? And then they talk about other things. And then they try to understand in a holistic way the situation of these people on the ground. And if they see there are problems that they can help solving whether it brings them profit or not, doesn't matter because they were managing the relationship.

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: ¿Right?

entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: And the relationship is a very important asset. And I think this is why my interpretation is, they've been to chaos and back. And they know that if you collaborate with people, then you'll manage to solve the situation. If you have an antagonistic relationship with them or just, you know, strategic, instrumental, transactional, then you're. It will not work. People will not get out of their way to help you if you're not getting out of your way to help them now. ¿Right? And they're in power position, but the day there's a problem, they will need to have the collective intelligence and help. And they're nurturing this. They are nurturing their environments beyond their own immediate interest. And in doing this, they are not homo economicus. We are seeing people who seem to be full human beings and not just homo economicus. There is something more in the relation that they have on the field that we have seen, or even together, that they do not act only as the instantaneous self utility maximizing economic actor. There is more than that. And in this perspective, they have a systemic view. They have a larger view than the economic transaction, and that's sustainability. As we see, it long term understanding of the health of the whole system. And the health is not just economic. It's technical and it's social. And they take that into account. But they do it not like we do it. Here by the book like we look. Okay. ¿What's the pillar number one, pillar number three? Pillar number three. That's not how it happens. These guys are trying to see from the perspective of their stakeholders what problem they have sincerely. And they try to see if they can solve it with what they have. And if they can they do. Of course, they still are aware of their own limits. And that at the end of the year, they will have to justify that they have not wasted much money and that everything works, etcetera. But if they can't do more, they will do more. And they will not be expecting to have something in return because they've given more. This is just my projection. I don't know. But I felt it like this for us. Who are used to discussing with people in the West and who are business, you know, that is not the way they operate. This is not business, as we say it. ¿This is activity, right?

entries.speaker: Sofia
entries.text: Yes. I was thinking two things that might be interesting to follow up. Because I know you will have. Yes, two things. And I was looking a little bit at the history of the company. I don't know if and how we could get something in those lines, but I feel there's something there. Because in the interviews, the people were young and the others had joined the company no more than ten years ago. And so on. So we got a vision of the last few years. But I was looking that this company has been around for one hundred twenty years. So they have suffered all the violence waves and then narco traffic and then whatever else. And they have become the. It's the largest drink company in the country and one of the largest of South America. And trying to understand a little bit this. And this resilience or chaos and back and so on, would be very interesting. At least a little bit, or at least how they. How they see today. Because there is a sense of this chaos today. Or the possibility of this chaos. Which is not that they are feeling it right now. It's rather something that could happen again, but rest. Trying to understand that a little bit. And the other thing which relates to the message from Paulo sent in the chat is the relationship of these with the rest of the conglomerate. They are a part of. So it's not only the twelve companies, but the person that owns this is an organization with his name. And it's one of the richest persons in the country. They have eighty companies and more interestingly, maybe they have one of the largest sugar producing companies and one of the largest radio and television groups as well. So this would be interesting as well.

entries.speaker: Paulius

So you have the efficacy that if we do a prayer like this, it's still going to be okay and profitable. But seeing before that, I mean, this sounds crazy if you just think in terms of business, in the classic economic approach, you would never do this. You would just do the minimum and try to challenge various producers to get the best price exactly what they're doing in the west. So there must have been some experience that made them change their ways. I think it's absolutely crucial that these guys are a family owned business. The three that we saw that had the same strategy, I mean, Luker, Waffle, Krebs and this one. They are all family run business, even though they're big. And the influence of the founder is very important. And the importance of people, it was very clear that smarta the director for sustainability. This is a strong personality. And she was very, very important in all the creation of this. So she must have the trust of the boss somewhere. It's not easy. And obviously, she's doing a lot of work and she was instrumental. So she's wow, she's a woman that, you know, she is good and smart and. And works hard. Okay.

entries.speaker: Sofia
