entries.speaker: Paulius
entries.text: Yeah, you see, one thing that happened is that in the beginning, Martyr was with us. Right. And so they had not planned that we would stay seven hours. Right. They probably thought the beginning, the morning and the idea was, Martha is going to tell us the usual shebang about sustainability, blah, blah, blah, etc. And then she would leave us with the Foundation. And then we get, you know, they would give us some bottles of poster burn. And then tote bag and everything would be fine. And after she had talked for fifteen minutes, I was very impolite. I stopped her presentation. Say, you know, this is not what we want. I'm sorry. We want precise fact, why you're doing this, etcetera, etc. And I think she was upset. And she was right to be so. And so I think she's still a bit upset. And that's why she left. But she would have left anyway. But this puts the discussion on a completely different track. Because they started to understand what we were after. Like, we're not journalists. And even though, I mean, they had seen us before, you know, we did not talk with them. As we are coming here to listen, it's like we're coming here to discuss. And so I'm afraid I broke a bit the relationship with Marta. ¿So next time it's better be who goes, right? But in any case, we had a very good, very, very detailed discussion. With these guys. From the Sustainability Foundation. From the Foundation. And actually, they started giving us in the discussion. They shared their Excel spreadsheet With the real figures and the names and all the rest of it. So this was not recorded, but were here. And then, well, the talk was recorded. So this is really typically not the kind of stuff. ¿That they perhaps should have shared, right? Because they trusted us. But, you know, because we asked very, very specific questions. And the guys were there and they just shared it. And so we started to understand That they actually have a very, very precise idea. Of how much this cost. So this is not like they're naive and giving away money. They know what they're doing. And they are aware of how much it costs. And they go anyway. And so what we would like to do with Palius when we go back, there is. But we need to have a conversation with them beforehand is saying, look, we love what you're doing. Guys, this is really good. And we believe that. I mean, I'm sincere when I say this. ¿I say, can we sit down together and have a look at the finances of that stuff? And then we write a paper together. And we show that it is worth it to play the sustainability game, at least in Colombia. And we need their help, and not just their help. We need their. In a way, their commitment and their consent to do this. And we're not going to do this in a way that will not be detrimental to them. But they must trust us in the way we do this. And I think this is what we should have. Because I've never seen such figures. And I've never seen such figures of company. That is actually sustainable. I mean, these guys play the game for real and they win so. And I think it's exactly the same with Krebs and with Luker. And so it turns out these guys can do this because there's no pressure from their shareholders. ¿Every quarter say, How much money did you bring? ¿And a pension fan say, how come you pay ten percent more for the fruits than your competitors? But the proof is, in the pudding, these guys make ten percent growth on average. So that is what we have to figure out. ¿The big question is, is this also valid in less risky environments? This I don't know. Because what you do when you do investments in social capital is actually that you save transaction costs in the future. And that's exactly the nature of what an organization is, according to a Coz and Williamson. So an organization is a way to set up, you know, processes. So you do not have to haggle and discuss every time and you save transaction costs. And that's exactly what they're doing. They're discussing with them the quantity, the quality, the format, the dates and everything just as if they were in the organization. Except that they do not hold the risk of having them inside. So they have externalized part of the risk, but not everything. And here we externalize everything of the risk because we know there's not much risk. But they have a different approach. They share the risk in a way with their partners and they pay for that. The fact, I was flabbergasted to realize that these guys are paying the insurance for the crops.

Because they're so big that they can do that. So I don't know if in a different context you can actually spend the way they do to kind of invest in their future, which is great, but maybe they don't behave like capitalists because they are not. They're basically, you know, plutocrast, plutocrist or whatever. I don't know how to say that, but they have absolute control of everything.
